frameworks.name: cost of action selection
frameworks.one_line: Choosing actions incurs physical and mental effort costs that observers infer.

frameworks.name: intuitive psychology
frameworks.one_line: Children reason about others by attributing utilities and costs to actions.

frameworks.name: developmental cognition
frameworks.one_line: Tests whether preschoolers already use adult-like effort-cost inference.

core_question: Do preschoolers incorporate mental effort, alongside physical effort, into their intuitive understanding of why others act as they do?
key_assumptions: Action understanding involves attributing costs to bodies and minds
Mazes of varying complexity index physical and mental effort
4- to 5-year-olds can reason over effort-vs-gain trade-offs
manipulations: Goal type (reach external reward vs practice maze-solving)
key_findings: Children were sensitive to physical effort imposed by complex mazes
Children were sensitive to mental effort imposed by complex mazes
Children tracked trade-offs between effort and gain in skill
interpretations: The intuition that choices impose costs on bodies and minds guides children's action understanding
Mental-effort cost is part of early intuitive psychology, not a late acquisition
